**Contemporary classical music** is [Western art music](/wiki/Western_art_music "Western art music") composed close to the present day. At the beginning of the [21st century](/wiki/21st-century_classical_music "21st-century classical music"), it commonly referred to the post-1945 [post-tonal](/wiki/Modernism_(music) "Modernism (music)") music after the death of [Anton Webern](/wiki/Anton_Webern "Anton Webern"), and included [serial music](/wiki/Serial_music "Serial music"), [electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music"), [experimental music](/wiki/Experimental_music "Experimental music"), and [minimalist music](/wiki/Minimalist_music "Minimalist music"). Newer forms include [spectral music](/wiki/Spectral_music "Spectral music") and [post-minimalism](/wiki/Postminimalism#Music "Postminimalism").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contemporary_classical_music&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contemporary_classical_music&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

Main article: [20th-century classical music](/wiki/20th-century_classical_music "20th-century classical music")

At the beginning of the 20th century, composers of [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") were experimenting with an increasingly [dissonant](/wiki/Consonance_and_dissonance "Consonance and dissonance") pitch language, which sometimes yielded [atonal](/wiki/Atonality "Atonality") pieces. Following [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), as a backlash against what they saw as the increasingly exaggerated gestures and formlessness of late [Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism"), certain composers adopted a [neoclassic](/wiki/Neoclassicism_(music) "Neoclassicism (music)") style, which sought to recapture the balanced forms and clearly perceptible thematic processes of earlier styles[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhittall2001-1) (see also [New Objectivity](/wiki/New_Objectivity "New Objectivity") and [social realism](/wiki/Social_realism "Social realism")). In the [post-war era](/wiki/Post-war_era "Post-war era"), modernist composers sought to achieve greater levels of control in their composition process (e.g., through the use of the [twelve-tone technique](/wiki/Twelve-tone_technique "Twelve-tone technique") and later total [serialism](/wiki/Serialism "Serialism")). At the same time, conversely, composers also experimented with means of abdicating control, exploring indeterminacy or aleatoric processes in smaller or larger degrees.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwartzGodfrey1993ch._7:_"Order_and_Chaos",_pp._78ff-2) Technological advances led to the birth of electronic music.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning200419ff-3) Experimentation with tape loops and repetitive textures contributed to the advent of [minimalism](/wiki/Minimalism "Minimalism").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwartzGodfrey1993325-4) Still other composers started exploring the theatrical potential of the musical performance ([performance art](/wiki/Performance_art "Performance art"), [mixed media](/wiki/Mixed_media "Mixed media"), [fluxus](/wiki/Fluxus "Fluxus")).[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwartzGodfrey1993289ff-5) New works of contemporary classical music continue to be created. Each year, the [Boston Conservatory at Berklee](/wiki/Boston_Conservatory_at_Berklee "Boston Conservatory at Berklee") presents 700 performances. New works from contemporary classical music program students comprise roughly 150 of these performances.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### 1945–75

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contemporary_classical_music&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1945–75")]

To some extent, European and American traditions diverged after World War II. Among the most influential composers in Europe were [Pierre Boulez](/wiki/Pierre_Boulez "Pierre Boulez"), [Luigi Nono](/wiki/Luigi_Nono "Luigi Nono"), and [Karlheinz Stockhausen](/wiki/Karlheinz_Stockhausen "Karlheinz Stockhausen"). The first and last were both pupils of [Olivier Messiaen](/wiki/Olivier_Messiaen "Olivier Messiaen"). An important aesthetic philosophy as well as a group of compositional techniques at this time was [serialism](/wiki/Serialism "Serialism") (also called "through-ordered music", "'total' music" or "total tone ordering"), which took as its starting point the compositions of [Arnold Schoenberg](/wiki/Arnold_Schoenberg "Arnold Schoenberg") and [Anton Webern](/wiki/Anton_Webern "Anton Webern") (and thus was opposed to traditional twelve-tone music), and was also closely related to [Le Corbusier](/wiki/Le_Corbusier "Le Corbusier")'s idea of the *[modulor](/wiki/Modulor "Modulor")*.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBandur20015,_10–11-7) However, some more traditionally based composers such as [Dmitri Shostakovich](/wiki/Dmitri_Shostakovich "Dmitri Shostakovich") and [Benjamin Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten") maintained a tonal style of composition despite the prominent serialist movement.

In America, composers like [Milton Babbitt](/wiki/Milton_Babbitt "Milton Babbitt"), [John Cage](/wiki/John_Cage "John Cage"), [Elliott Carter](/wiki/Elliott_Carter "Elliott Carter"), [Henry Cowell](/wiki/Henry_Cowell "Henry Cowell"), [Philip Glass](/wiki/Philip_Glass "Philip Glass"), [Steve Reich](/wiki/Steve_Reich "Steve Reich"), [George Rochberg](/wiki/George_Rochberg "George Rochberg"), and [Roger Sessions](/wiki/Roger_Sessions "Roger Sessions") formed their own ideas. Some of these composers (Cage, Cowell, Glass, Reich) represented a new methodology of [experimental music](/wiki/Experimental_music "Experimental music"), which began to question fundamental notions of music such as [notation](/wiki/Musical_notation "Musical notation"), [performance](/wiki/Performance "Performance"), duration, and repetition, while others (Babbitt, Rochberg, Sessions) fashioned their own extensions of the twelve-tone serialism of [Schoenberg](/wiki/Arnold_Schoenberg "Arnold Schoenberg").

## Movements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contemporary_classical_music&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Movements")]

### Neoromanticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contemporary_classical_music&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Neoromanticism")]

Main article: [Neoromanticism (music)](/wiki/Neoromanticism_(music) "Neoromanticism (music)")

The vocabulary of extended tonality, which flourished in the late 19th and very early 20th centuries, continues to be used by contemporary composers. It has never been considered shocking or controversial in the larger musical world—as has been demonstrated statistically for the United States, at least, where "most composers continued working in what has remained throughout this century the mainstream of tonal-oriented composition".[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStraus1999303,_307–308,_310–11,_314–329-8)

### High modernism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contemporary_classical_music&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: High modernism")]

Main article: [High modernism](/wiki/High_modernism "High modernism")

[Serialism](/wiki/Serialism "Serialism") is one of the most important post-war movements among the high modernist schools. Serialism, more specifically named "integral" or "compound" serialism, was led by composers such as [Pierre Boulez](/wiki/Pierre_Boulez "Pierre Boulez"), [Luciano Berio](/wiki/Luciano_Berio "Luciano Berio"), [Bruno Maderna](/wiki/Bruno_Maderna "Bruno Maderna"), [Luigi Nono](/wiki/Luigi_Nono "Luigi Nono"), and [Karlheinz Stockhausen](/wiki/Karlheinz_Stockhausen "Karlheinz Stockhausen") in Europe, and by [Milton Babbitt](/wiki/Milton_Babbitt "Milton Babbitt"), [Donald Martino](/wiki/Donald_Martino "Donald Martino"), [Mario Davidovsky](/wiki/Mario_Davidovsky "Mario Davidovsky"), and [Charles Wuorinen](/wiki/Charles_Wuorinen "Charles Wuorinen") in the United States. Some of their compositions use an ordered set or several such sets, which may be the basis for the whole composition, while others use "unordered" sets. The term is also often used for [dodecaphony](/wiki/Dodecaphony "Dodecaphony"), or [twelve-tone technique](/wiki/Twelve-tone_technique "Twelve-tone technique"), which is alternatively regarded as the model for integral serialism.

Despite its decline in the last third of the 20th century, there remained at the end of the century an active core of composers who continued to advance the ideas and forms of high modernism. Those no longer living include [Pierre Boulez](/wiki/Pierre_Boulez "Pierre Boulez"), [Pauline Oliveros](/wiki/Pauline_Oliveros "Pauline Oliveros"), [Toru Takemitsu](/wiki/Toru_Takemitsu "Toru Takemitsu"), [Jacob Druckman](/wiki/Jacob_Druckman "Jacob Druckman"), [George Perle](/wiki/George_Perle "George Perle"), [Ralph Shapey](/wiki/Ralph_Shapey "Ralph Shapey"),[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBotstein2001§9-9) [Franco Donatoni](/wiki/Franco_Donatoni "Franco Donatoni"), [Wolfgang Rihm](/wiki/Wolfgang_Rihm "Wolfgang Rihm"), [Jonathan Harvey](/wiki/Jonathan_Harvey_(composer) "Jonathan Harvey (composer)"),[[10]](#cite_note-schwartz-10) [Erkki Salmenhaara](/wiki/Erkki_Salmenhaara "Erkki Salmenhaara"), [Henrik Otto Donner](/wiki/Henrik_Otto_Donner "Henrik Otto Donner") and [Richard Wernick](/wiki/Richard_Wernick "Richard Wernick").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson199218-11) Those still living in October 2025 include [Helmut Lachenmann](/wiki/Helmut_Lachenmann "Helmut Lachenmann"), [Salvatore Sciarrino](/wiki/Salvatore_Sciarrino "Salvatore Sciarrino"), [Magnus Lindberg](/wiki/Magnus_Lindberg_(Finnish_composer) "Magnus Lindberg (Finnish composer)"),[[10]](#cite_note-schwartz-10) [George Benjamin](/wiki/George_Benjamin_(composer) "George Benjamin (composer)"), [Brian Ferneyhough](/wiki/Brian_Ferneyhough "Brian Ferneyhough"), [Richard Wilson](/wiki/Richard_Edward_Wilson "Richard Edward Wilson"), and [James MacMillan](/wiki/James_MacMillan "James MacMillan").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson2001-12)

##### Computer music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contemporary_classical_music&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Computer music")]

Main article: [Computer music](/wiki/Computer_music "Computer music")

Between 1975 and 1990, a shift in the paradigm of [computer](/wiki/Computer "Computer") technology had taken place, making electronic music systems affordable and widely accessible. The personal computer had become an essential component of the electronic musician's equipment, superseding [analog](/wiki/Analog_synthesizer "Analog synthesizer") [synthesizers](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer") and fulfilling the traditional functions of composition and scoring, synthesis and sound processing, sampling of audio input, and control over external equipment.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolmes2008272-13)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

#### Historicism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contemporary_classical_music&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Historicism")]

[Musical historicism](/wiki/Musical_historicism "Musical historicism")—the use of historical materials, structures, styles, techniques, media, conceptual content, etc., whether by a single composer or those associated with a particular school, movement, or period—is evident to varying degrees in minimalism, post-minimalism, world-music, and other genres in which tonal traditions have been sustained or have undergone a significant revival in recent decades.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatkins1994440–442,_446–448-15) Some post-minimalist works employ medieval and other genres associated with early music, such as the "Oi me lasso" and other [laude](/wiki/Laude "Laude") of [Gavin Bryars](/wiki/Gavin_Bryars "Gavin Bryars").

The historicist movement is closely related to the emergence of musicology and the [early music revival](/wiki/Early_music_revival "Early music revival"). A number of historicist composers have been influenced by their intimate familiarity with the instrumental practices of earlier periods ([Hendrik Bouman](/wiki/Hendrik_Bouman "Hendrik Bouman"), Grant Colburn, [Michael Talbot](/wiki/Michael_Talbot_(musicologist) "Michael Talbot (musicologist)"), [Paulo Galvão](/wiki/Paulo_Galv%C3%A3o "Paulo Galvão"), [Roman Turovsky-Savchuk](/wiki/Roman_Turovsky-Savchuk "Roman Turovsky-Savchuk")). The musical historicism movement has also been stimulated by the formation of such international organizations as the [Delian Society](/wiki/Delian_Society "Delian Society") and [Vox Saeculorum](/wiki/Vox_Saeculorum "Vox Saeculorum").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColburn200736–45,_54–55-16)

#### New Complexity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contemporary_classical_music&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: New Complexity")]

[New Complexity](/wiki/New_Complexity "New Complexity") is a current within today's[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] European contemporary avant-garde music scene, named in reaction to the New Simplicity. Amongst the candidates suggested for having coined the term are the composer [Nigel Osborne](/wiki/Nigel_Osborne "Nigel Osborne"), the Belgian musicologist [Harry Halbreich](/wiki/Harry_Halbreich "Harry Halbreich"), and the British/Australian musicologist [Richard Toop](/wiki/Richard_Toop "Richard Toop"), who gave currency to the concept of a movement with his article "Four Facets of the New Complexity".[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEToop1988-18)

Though often [atonal](/wiki/Atonal "Atonal"), highly abstract, and [dissonant](/wiki/Consonance_and_dissonance "Consonance and dissonance") in sound, the "New Complexity" is most readily characterized by the use of techniques which require complex [musical notation](/wiki/Musical_notation "Musical notation"). This includes [extended techniques](/wiki/Extended_technique "Extended technique"), [microtonality](/wiki/Microtonality "Microtonality"), odd [tunings](/wiki/Musical_tuning "Musical tuning"), highly disjunct [melodic contour](/wiki/Melodic_motion "Melodic motion"), innovative [timbres](/wiki/Timbre "Timbre"), complex [polyrhythms](/wiki/Polyrhythms "Polyrhythms"), unconventional [instrumentations](/wiki/Instrumentation_(music) "Instrumentation (music)"), abrupt changes in loudness and intensity, and so on.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The diverse group of composers writing in this style includes [Richard Barrett](/wiki/Richard_Barrett_(composer) "Richard Barrett (composer)"), [Brian Ferneyhough](/wiki/Brian_Ferneyhough "Brian Ferneyhough"), [Claus-Steffen Mahnkopf](/wiki/Claus-Steffen_Mahnkopf "Claus-Steffen Mahnkopf"), [James Dillon](/wiki/James_Dillon_(composer) "James Dillon (composer)"), [Michael Finnissy](/wiki/Michael_Finnissy "Michael Finnissy"), [James Erber](/wiki/James_Erber "James Erber"), and [Roger Redgate](/wiki/Roger_Redgate "Roger Redgate").

#### Ambient music and its crossovers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Contemporary_classical_music&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Ambient music and its crossovers")]

Modern ambient music blends classical, electronic, and minimalism, driven by artists like [Jon Hopkins](/wiki/Jon_Hopkins "Jon Hopkins"), [Erland Cooper](/wiki/Erland_Cooper "Erland Cooper"), [Max Richter](/wiki/Max_Richter "Max Richter"), [Richard D. James](/wiki/Aphex_Twin "Aphex Twin"), [Ludovico Einaudi](/wiki/Ludovico_Einaudi "Ludovico Einaudi"), [Nils Frahm](/wiki/Nils_Frahm "Nils Frahm"), [Ólafur Arnalds](/wiki/%C3%93lafur_Arnalds "Ólafur Arnalds"), [Lambert](/wiki/Lambert_(pianist) "Lambert (pianist)"), [Joep Beving](/wiki/Joep_Beving "Joep Beving"), and [Hania Rani](/wiki/Hania_Rani "Hania Rani").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Influenced by [Brian Eno](/wiki/Brian_Eno "Brian Eno") and [Steve Reich](/wiki/Steve_Reich "Steve Reich"), this genre, sometimes called "neo-classical" or "indie classical", merges cinematic orchestration with electronic textures, appealing to a broader audience.[[22]](#cite_note-:0-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Labels like [Erased Tapes Records](/wiki/Erased_Tapes_Records "Erased Tapes Records"), [New Amsterdam Records](/wiki/New_Amsterdam_Records "New Amsterdam Records"), and [130701](/wiki/FatCat_Records "FatCat Records") have played a key role in this movement, while BBC Radio 3 and BBC Radio 6 Music have promoted its popularity. Programs by Ólafur Arnalds and Mary Anne Hobbs highlight the fusion of ambient, classical, and experimental soundscapes.[[22]](#cite_note-:0-22)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)
